talk_title: Stromal microenvironment signatures predict bone metastasis in prostate cancer

speaker.name: Massimo Loda
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medical Center, Department of Pathology, New York, USA

biomarkers.name: periostin expression
biomarkers.finding: higher periostin in stromal fibroblasts correlates with neuroendocrine differentiation and bone metastasis

models.name: TMPRSS2-ERG, PTEN knockout, MYC high, N-Myc overexpression prostate models
models.genotype_or_key_features: various oncogenic alterations driving progressive aggressiveness

MOA_summary: Loda's group identified a bone‑like stromal gene signature in primary prostate tumors, driven by periostin‑rich cancer‑associated fibroblasts that arise via epithelial‑to‑stromal signaling (loss of AR motifs, gain of AP1 motifs) and promote neuroendocrine differentiation and bone organotropism.
key_findings: A stromal osteomimicry signature, including periostin, distinguishes high‑grade prostate tumors that metastasize to bone.
Periostin is enriched in neuroendocrine prostate cancer stroma and colocalizes with αVβ3/αVβ5 integrin signaling.
Mouse models recapitulate human stromal changes; fibroblast clusters acquire AP1 motifs and lose AR/GR motifs.
Co‑culture of periostin‑expressing fibroblasts or recombinant periostin induces neuroendocrine differentiation (FOXA2) in organoids.
Spatial analysis shows reduced distance between periostin‑positive fibroblasts and neuroendocrine cells in bone metastases versus primary tumors.
open_questions: Can periostin inhibition prevent neuroendocrine transdifferentiation and bone metastasis in vivo?
What are the distinct ECM compositions produced by periostin‑high versus periostin‑low fibroblast clusters?

talk_title: Metabolic dependencies drive organ‑specific metastasis in breast cancer

speaker.name: George Poulogiannis
speaker.affiliation: Institute of Cancer Research, Division of Cell and Molecular Biology, London, UK

biomarkers.name: glutamine uptake
biomarkers.finding: bone‑metastatic cells show higher glutamine uptake and increased M+4 labeling of TCA intermediates

models.name: TRAP neuroendocrine prostate model (used for stromal comparison)

MOA_summary: Poulogiannis demonstrated that organ‑specific metabolic environments create unique dependencies: brain metastases rely on de novo fatty‑acid synthesis (FASN), lung metastases on pyruvate carboxylase, and bone metastases on glutaminolysis (GLS1), making these enzymes tractable therapeutic targets.
key_findings: Metabolic gene signatures predict overall metastatic potential with 86% accuracy; metabolic‑only features improve prediction.
Organ‑specific dependencies identified: FASN in brain, PC in lung, GLS1 in bone metastases.
Bone‑metastatic clones exhibit heightened glutamine uptake, increased GLS1 activity, and sensitivity to GLS1 inhibitor CBA39.
Depleting serine/glycine reduces metastasis in bone, kidney, ovary but not in brain, liver, lung.
Integrated MetaboForm platform infers tumor metabolomes from transcriptomics, revealing distinct metabolic landscapes across metastatic sites.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: GLS1 inhibition shows specificity for bone‑derived clones; transporter inhibition (SLC1A5, SLC38A12) ineffective due to compensatory uptake.

combination_strategies.rationale: lung metastatic cells depend on serine‑derived α‑KG to sustain mTOR signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced sensitivity of lung clones to rapamycin when pyruvate carboxylase is inhibited

open_questions: Can metabolic dependencies be exploited clinically to prevent organ‑specific colonization?
What determines the heterogeneity of metabolic phenotypes within a single patient’s metastatic lesions?

talk_title: CTC heterogeneity and TMPO as a driver of metastasis in prostate cancer

speaker.name: Cory Barret Shen
speaker.affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Medicine and Molecular Pharmacology and Systems Biology, New York, USA

biomarkers.name: TMPO expression
biomarkers.finding: high TMPO in castration‑resistant prostate CTCs correlates with increased CTC count and bone metastasis

models.name: Prostate cancer GEM models with lineage‑traceable fluorescent markers
CTC‑derived organoids (iOrganoids)
models.genotype_or_key_features: derived from single circulating tumor cells, retain primary tumor phenotype

MOA_summary: Shen showed that TMPO (LAP2) sustains a hypoxia‑driven transcriptional program in large CTC‑derived organoids, promoting survival in circulation and bone colonization; TMPO knockdown shifts cells toward oxidative phosphorylation, reduces organoid size, CTC numbers, and bone metastasis without affecting primary tumor growth.
key_findings: CTC burden correlates tightly (R>0.9) with metastatic score in GEM mice.
Single CTCs can be cultured into organoids; organoid size segregates into hypoxic (large) vs. ox‑phos (small) phenotypes.
TMPO knockdown does not affect primary tumor growth but reduces circulating CTCs and bone metastasis in both subcutaneous and intracardiac models.
TMPO activity correlates with hypoxia signatures and organoid size; loss of TMPO shifts metabolism toward oxidative phosphorylation.
Human CRPC CTCs show high nuclear TMPO co‑expressed with lamin, supporting clinical relevance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TMPO inhibition selectively impairs hypoxia‑adapted CTCs while sparing ox‑phos clones.

combination_strategies.rationale: bone‑metastatic CTCs rely on glutamine; TMPO loss reduces hypoxia‑driven glutamine dependence
combination_strategies.phenotype: additive reduction of bone metastasis in mouse models

open_questions: Does TMPO regulate dormancy versus active dissemination in CTCs?
What upstream signals control TMPO expression in circulating cells?
